message.content:
You are verifier #2, independent. Do NOT optimistically confirm. Read the ACTUAL code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay and independently determine whether this is a TRUE production-readiness problem in the CURRENT code (3 hardening passes already applied).

Finding G-020: make_gate_node re-raises a recorder failure after the interrupt resume was already consumed (stranded-resume bricking)
File: src/assay_engine/orchestration/gatenode.py:145
Description: In the gate node resume loop, once apply_gate_decision validates a resume value the node calls recorder(rec) (gatenode.py:145) inside the still-open while True loop, then returns. interrupt() (:137) has already consumed and persisted the operator's resume value into the checkpoint before validation. If recorder raises (trail clock raises, payload unserializable, provenance lock contended — all real ProvenanceError modes) the exception propagates out of node(). The module's own rationale (docstring 16-21, inline 133-135) is that a raise after the resume is consumed strands the value in the checkpoint and replays it forever, which is why a BAD resume re-fires interrupt instead of raising. A recorder failure on a GOOD resume hits exactly that hazard: the validated decision is lost, and on replay the node re-fires interrupt (already_decided False, recorder never wrote the terminal record), re-prompting the operator while the prior valid resume index is gone.
PoC: def flaky_recorder(rec): raise RuntimeError('provenance trail unavailable'). make_gate_node(gate, propose=..., recorder=flaky_recorder); operator submits a valid 'approved' resume; recorder raises -> exception escapes node(); the approved resume was already persisted by interrupt() and is lost on replay.
Suggested fix: Make the in-loop recorder call resilient like the rest of the protocol: either attempt-record-then-return normalizing a recorder failure into a re-fired interrupt carrying the error, or record best-effort with a structured warning (mirroring pipeline.py:738-747 'never mask / never strand'). Do not let a recorder exception escape after interrupt() has consumed the resume value.

Confirmed=true ONLY if the problem exists as described in current code, the PoC is reproducible, and it is a genuine concern (not speculative, not already-handled, not intended design). Be concrete and cite file:line.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	"""Human-in-the-loop gate node — the interrupt/resume protocol (GOVERNANCE.md).
2	
3	:func:`make_gate_node` turns an engine :class:`~assay_engine.orchestration.gates.Gate` plus a
4	proposal builder into a LangGraph node that pauses the run at the gate (``interrupt``),
5	surfaces a proposal to the operator, and on resume validates and records the decision.
6	
7	The **gate-correlation guard** rejects a resumed decision aimed at a *different* gate than the
8	one currently parked — a stale or misrouted decision (e.g. delivered late through the
9	governance channel) must not be applied to whatever gate happens to be waiting. The guard's
10	expected gate is the gate the operator named in the resume value, compared against this node's
11	gate; auto-deriving it from the parked gate would make the guard always match and thus a no-op.
12	
13	Two further hardened behaviors, ported faithfully from the prior platform's gate node:
14	
15	- **Re-prompt on a bad resume (not raise).** ``interrupt()`` consumes and *persists* the
16	  resume value into the checkpoint *before* the node returns, so raising on a malformed or
17	  mis-correlated value would strand it in the checkpoint and replay it forever — the gate
18	  would be permanently bricked. Instead the node validates inside a loop and re-fires
19	  ``interrupt()`` (carrying the error) on a bad value, so the operator can re-submit and
20	  actually recover.
21	- **Terminal-decision idempotency.** An ``approved`` decision is terminal: re-entering an
22	  already-approved gate is a no-op (it does not re-prompt or re-record). ``rejected`` and
23	  ``deferred`` are deliberately NOT terminal, so a reject→revise→re-review loop re-fires the
24	  interrupt as intended.
25	
26	The node returns a partial state update appending one record to ``gate_decisions``; a study's
27	graph state should declare ``gate_decisions`` with a list/add reducer so records accumulate
28	across gates. The decision/recording validator (:func:`apply_gate_decision`) is pure and
29	tested offline; only the ``interrupt`` call requires LangGraph (the optional ``orchestration``
30	extra, imported lazily).
31	"""
32	
33	from __future__ import annotations
34	
35	import warnings
36	from typing import Any, Callable, Mapping
37	
38	from assay_engine.orchestration.gates import Gate, GateError
39	
40	GateProposal = Callable[[Mapping[str, Any]], Mapping[str, Any]]
41	
42	_VALID_DECISIONS = frozenset({"approved", "rejected", "deferred"})
43	
44	
45	def apply_gate_decision(gate: Gate, resumed: Any) -> dict[str, Any]:
46	    """Validate a resumed operator decision and return the state update to record it.
47	
48	    Raises :class:`GateError` if the resume value is malformed, targets a different gate
49	    (correlation guard), or carries an unrecognized decision.
50	    """
51	    if not isinstance(resumed, Mapping):
52	        raise GateError(
53	            f"gate {gate.name!r}: resume value must be a mapping, got {type(resumed).__name__}"
54	        )
55	    target = resumed.get("gate_id")
56	    if target != gate.name:
57	        raise GateError(
58	            f"gate {gate.name!r}: resumed decision targets {target!r} — stale or misrouted; "
59	            "rejecting (gate-correlation guard)"
60	        )
61	    decision = str(resumed.get("decision", "")).strip().lower()
62	    if decision not in _VALID_DECISIONS:
63	        raise GateError(
64	            f"gate {gate.name!r}: invalid decision {decision!r} "
65	            f"(expected one of {sorted(_VALID_DECISIONS)})"
66	        )
67	    # A rejection/deferral with no rationale is governance-relevant; record a sentinel rather
68	    # than a blank (incl. whitespace-only) so the audit trail is never silently empty.
69	    rationale = str(resumed.get("rationale") or "").strip() or "No rationale provided."
70	    record = {"gate": gate.name, "decision": decision, "rationale": rationale}
71	    return {"gate_decisions": [record]}
72	
73	
74	# Only an approval is terminal: a re-entered approved gate is a no-op, but a rejected/deferred
75	# gate must re-fire its interrupt so a revise→re-review loop can re-prompt the operator.
76	_TERMINAL_DECISIONS = frozenset({"approved"})
77	
78	
79	def already_decided(state: Mapping[str, Any], gate_name: str) -> bool:
80	    """True if ``gate_name`` already holds a terminal (approved) decision in ``gate_decisions``."""
81	    for record in state.get("gate_decisions", []) or []:
82	        if record.get("gate") == gate_name and record.get("decision") in _TERMINAL_DECISIONS:
83	            return True
84	    return False
85	
86	
87	GateRecorder = Callable[[Mapping[str, Any]], None]
88	
89	
90	def make_gate_node(
91	    gate: Gate,
92	    propose: GateProposal,
93	    *,
94	    recorder: GateRecorder | None = None,
95	) -> Callable[[Mapping[str, Any]], dict[str, Any]]:
96	    """Build a LangGraph node that parks at ``gate`` for an operator decision.
97	
98	    ``propose(state)`` returns the proposal payload surfaced to the operator (what the gate is
99	    asking them to approve). The node returns a ``{"gate_decisions": [record]}`` update, or
100	    ``{}`` (no-op) if the gate is already terminally approved.
101	
102	    ``recorder`` (optional) bridges each validated decision into the hash-chained provenance
103	    trail (#111): the durable LangGraph path otherwise records gate decisions only as
104	    (untamper-evident) graph state, so the documented append-only trail would miss them. Pass a
105	    callback such as ``lambda r: trail.record("gate", f"gate {r['gate']}: {r['decision']}", **r)``
106	    so the graph path's approvals/rejections land in the same chained trail as the runner's.
107	
108	    Omitting ``recorder`` is a GOVERNANCE §3 audit gap: gate decisions then live only in
109	    (non-tamper-evident) LangGraph state, never in the hash-chained trail, so a checkpoint edit
110	    flipping ``rejected``→``approved`` is undetectable by ``verify_records``. Because that
111	    insecure configuration is effortless to ship by accident, building a node without a recorder
112	    emits a warning rather than failing silently (pass 3, #F-007).
113	    """
114	    if recorder is None:
115	        warnings.warn(
116	            f"make_gate_node({gate.name!r}) built with no recorder: gate decisions will NOT be "
117	            "written to the hash-chained provenance trail (GOVERNANCE §3 audit gap) — pass a "
118	            "recorder that calls trail.record('gate', ...) for a tamper-evident audit trail",
119	            stacklevel=2,
120	        )
121	
122	    def node(state: Mapping[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]:
123	        if already_decided(state, gate.name):
124	            return {}  # terminal decision exists — do not re-prompt or re-record
125	        try:
126	            from langgraph.types import interrupt
127	        except ImportError as exc:  # pragma: no cover - exercised only without the extra
128	            raise RuntimeError(
129	                "orchestration requires the 'orchestration' extra (langgraph) — not installed"
130	            ) from exc
131	        proposal = dict(propose(state))
132	        current: dict[str, Any] = {"gate_id": gate.name, "proposal": proposal}
133	        # Validate inside a loop: on a bad/mis-correlated resume, re-fire interrupt (next
134	        # resume index) carrying the error rather than raising — a raise would strand the
135	        # bad value in the checkpoint and replay it forever, bricking the gate.
136	        while True:
137	            resumed = interrupt(current)
138	            try:
139	                update = apply_gate_decision(gate, resumed)
140	            except GateError as exc:
141	                current = {"gate_id": gate.name, "proposal": proposal, "error": str(exc)}
142	                continue
143	            if recorder is not None:
144	                for rec in update.get("gate_decisions", []):
145	                    recorder(rec)  # land the decision in the hash-chained provenance trail
146	            return update
147	
148	    return node
149

content.text:
I have everything needed to render a verdict.

Verification summary:

The finding is accurate against the current code.

1. Code structure (gatenode.py:136-146): `interrupt(current)` at :137 consumes/persists the operator's resume value into the checkpoint. `apply_gate_decision` validates at :139. On a `GateError` (bad/mis-correlated resume) the loop re-fires interrupt (:140-142) — the documented "never strand" behavior. But on a GOOD resume, `recorder(rec)` is called at :145 inside the still-open `while True` loop. If `recorder` raises, the exception propagates straight out of `node()` — it is not caught.

2. The recorder can genuinely raise. `ProvenanceTrail.record()` (provenance.py:116) and the bound `as_recorder()._record` (:169-186) have multiple real raise paths: clock raises (:130), non-datetime/non-tz-aware clock (:133/138), unserializable payload (:143/156), booby-trapped decision attrs (:176). All surface as `ProvenanceError` (a `RuntimeError` subclass), which is not a `GateError`, so the `except GateError` at :140 does not catch it. Lock contention itself doesn't raise, but the clock/serialization modes are concrete.

3. The hazard is exactly the one the module's own docstring (lines 15-20) and inline comment (133-135) warn about: a raise after `interrupt()` has consumed the resume strands the value in the checkpoint. On replay, `already_decided` (:123) returns False (recorder never wrote the terminal record, and the returned `update` was never applied to graph state because the exception escaped before `return`), so the node re-fires `interrupt()` and re-prompts the operator. The validated approval is lost.

4. The PoC is reproducible: a `recorder` that raises `RuntimeError` on a valid `'approved'` resume causes the exception to escape `node()`.

5. The asymmetry is real and the fix direction is consistent with the codebase's own pattern (pipeline.py:738 "never mask the original failure"; the gate node already chose re-fire-interrupt over raise for the bad-resume case). The recorder-failure path on a good resume is the one branch that violates the "never strand" invariant.

This is a true production-readiness concern: data-loss/operator-re-prompt bricking on a recoverable infrastructure failure, in code whose entire stated design goal is to never strand a consumed resume. Severity is medium — it requires a recorder failure (operational, not attacker-controlled in the common case, though a hostile clock/payload could trigger it), the gate is not permanently bricked (operator can re-submit), and it degrades to re-prompting rather than silent corruption. It is not low because it directly contradicts the module's safety invariant and can lose a recorded governance approval.

input.reasoning: Confirmed in current code. gatenode.py:136-146: interrupt() at :137 consumes and persists the operator resume into the checkpoint BEFORE validation. The bad-resume branch correctly re-fires interrupt (except GateError, :140-142) to avoid stranding, but on a GOOD resume recorder(rec) is called at :145 inside the still-open while-True loop with no guard. A recorder exception is NOT a GateError, so it propagates straight out of node(). Recorder raises are real, not speculative: ProvenanceTrail.record() / as_recorder()._record (provenance.py:116, 169-186, called by gatenode) raise ProvenanceError (a RuntimeError, not GateError) on clock failure (:130), non-datetime/non-tz clock (:133/138), unserializable/un-freezable payload (:143/156), or booby-trapped decision attributes (:176). When that escapes node(), the returned update was never applied to graph state and the recorder never wrote the terminal record, so on replay already_decided() (:123) is False and the node re-fires interrupt, re-prompting the operator while the previously-consumed valid approval is lost in the checkpoint — exactly the strand-and-brick hazard the module docstring (lines 15-20) and inline comment (133-135) exist to prevent. The asymmetry (re-fire on bad resume, raise on recorder failure of a good resume) violates the module's own never-strand invariant, and the codebase already has the resilient pattern to mirror (pipeline.py:738 'never mask the original failure'). PoC reproducible: recorder=lambda r: (_ for _ in ()).throw(RuntimeError('provenance trail unavailable')); a valid 'approved' resume causes the exception to escape node() after interrupt consumed the resume. Severity medium not high: gate is not permanently bricked (operator can re-submit on the re-prompt), trigger is an operational/infra recorder fault rather than routine attacker input, and failure mode is lost-approval + re-prompt rather than silent tamper. Not low because it directly breaks the stated safety invariant and can drop a recorded governance approval.

content.text: Confirmed G-020 as a true medium-severity production-readiness concern. Details are in the StructuredOutput call above; the load-bearing location is /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/orchestration/gatenode.py:143-146 (unguarded `recorder(rec)` after `interrupt()` at :137 consumed the resume), with the real raise paths in /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/provenance.py:116-188.
